text: Last class we ran a post-mortem and explained what should have happened.
Today we compare how a process used to work and how it changed.

We decided to phase out the legacy system and streamline the workflow.
The rollout happened in stages, and throughput is far higher now.

Hi Fabiana, it is Klaus. Before our call, here is some background on how procurement evolved here. When I joined, the whole workflow was manual. We used to track every purchase order on paper, and every Friday we would reconcile the numbers by hand. It was cumbersome, and honestly, half of it is obsolete now. Then we started to phase out the legacy system. We did not flip a switch; the rollout happened in stages. First we ran a small pilot, then each iteration added more. We chose to automate the routine steps and streamline approvals, and we used a clear benchmark to measure progress. The transition was not easy. People had to get used to a new way of working, and the team needed time to ramp up. At one point we even had to pivot, because the first design did not fit how we actually order. Today the throughput is far higher than it used to be. So when we talk, I want you to compare the old way and the new way, and explain what we used to do, what we would do every week, and what you are getting used to now. Talk soon.

Hi team, here is my quick reflection on the transition. A year ago I used to spend half my week on manual data entry. Every Monday I would export the orders, clean them by hand, and email a report nobody read. That old workflow is obsolete now. When we started to phase out the legacy system, I was nervous. The rollout felt slow, and at first the new tool was almost as cumbersome as the old one. But each iteration got better. Once we decided to automate the routine steps and streamline the approvals, I finally started to get used to it. Now I am used to working inside the new system, and the throughput on my team has nearly doubled. If you are still ramping up, my advice is simple. Do not try to learn everything at once. Pick one process, adopt the new way, and let yourself get used to it before you move on. We had to pivot more than once, and that is normal. The point is that we kept improving.

First, the team used to run the whole workflow on paper, and every week they would reconcile the numbers by hand. Then they decided to phase out the legacy system and adopt a new one. The rollout happened in stages, one iteration at a time, so people could get used to it. They chose to automate the routine steps and streamline the approvals. At one point they had to pivot, because the first design did not fit. Today the throughput is far higher, and the old, cumbersome process is finally obsolete.
We are finally replacing the legacy system.
We will phase out the old tool by June.
We chose to automate the weekly report.
The old process was slow and cumbersome.
Each iteration was better than the last.
